ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general wonton entry; no specific dry-tossed shrimp-pork wonton with chicken sauce variant exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Wontons — stuffed pasta/dough product, seafood and meat filled
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for dry-tossed shrimp-pork wontons with chicken sauce. A0CKP (filled pasta/dough, mixed filling) is the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for this specific dish preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of dry-tossed shrimp and pork wontons is approximately 200 g (roughly 8–10 wontons with sauce), as commonly served at Singaporean hawker stalls and coffee shops.

nutrient_source.title: Shrimp & Pork Wantons Tossed In Well-Seasoned Chicken Sauce (Dry) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish (cr_id G00010054). All per-100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is not reported by HPB for this entry and is estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for comparable shrimp-pork wonton preparations (USDA SR Legacy #21349 and #21350 blended estimate); value set to 3.2 g/100g. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for dry-tossed shrimp-pork wontons. The HPB source reports carbohydrate as 0.0 g/100g for this dish entry, which is anomalous given that wonton wrappers are made from refined wheat flour. This likely reflects the HPB analytical result for the specific dish as tested (sauce-coated filling dominant in the sample). Because carbohydrate_g is reported as 0.0 g/100g by the approved source, glycemic load per serving calculates to 0.0. GI of 55 is assigned as an analogous estimate based on wheat-flour wonton wrappers per University of Sydney GI Database entry for pasta/dumpling-type wheat products. Per the standard GI classification scale (low: ≤55, medium: 56–69, high: ≥70), a GI of 55 falls in the low category. GL per serving = (55 × 0.0 g carb per 100g × 200g serving) / 100 = 0.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1050 mg/100g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold; driven by soy sauce, oyster sauce, and chicken stock in the seasoning sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; if consumed, request sauce on the side or reduced sauce, and avoid adding further soy sauce or chilli oil condiments, as a 200 g serving delivers approximately 2100 mg sodium — nearly the entire recommended daily limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Wonton wrappers are made from refined wheat flour with negligible dietary fibre (0 g/100g), contributing rapidly absorbed carbohydrates; the seasoning sauce may contain added sugar
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size to 4–5 wontons and request reduced or no added sugar in the sauce, as the refined wheat flour wrappers have a moderate-to-high glycaemic index and the sauce contributes additional simple sugars that can impair postprandial glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (1050 mg/100g) combined with moderate fat (10.5 g/100g) including saturated fat from pork, and cholesterol (89 mg/100g), presents a compounded cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat this dish as an occasional food only; the very high sodium content elevates blood pressure risk, while the saturated fat and cholesterol from pork filling contribute to atherogenic lipid profiles — request reduced sauce and smaller portions to mitigate risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork filling contributes arachidonic acid (omega-6), amplifying inflammatory cytokines; sodium at 1050 mg/100g worsens RA-related tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit or avoid this dish due to the pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid load from pork and the high sodium content that exacerbates joint swelling; consider requesting shrimp-only wontons with reduced sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dry-tossed shrimp and pork wontons (干拌虾肉馄饨) is a popular Singaporean Chinese hawker dish consisting of silky, thin wheat-flour wonton wrappers filled with a seasoned mixture of minced shrimp and pork. Unlike the soup version, the wontons are boiled and then drained and tossed dry in a well-seasoned chicken-based sauce that typically incorporates soy sauce, oyster sauce, sesame oil, and chilli oil, delivering a rich, savoury, and slightly spicy coating. The texture is smooth and tender with a satisfying bite from the shrimp-pork filling. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (19.1 g/100g) and moderate in fat (10.5 g/100g), with very high sodium content (1050 mg/100g) driven by the multi-component seasoning sauce. The HPB Singapore database reports carbohydrate as 0.0 g/100g for this preparation, likely reflecting the sauce-dominant analytical sample. Clinically, the dish is unsuitable for individuals with hypertension due to extreme sodium load, requires careful portion control for type 2 diabetes due to refined wheat flour wrappers, and poses cardiovascular risk from combined sodium, saturated fat, and cholesterol. It is not halal due to pork content. Commonly served at Singaporean hawker centres and coffee shops as a lunch or dinner option.
common_user_queries: What are the calories in dry wonton?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010054, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. The exact dish entry is present in HPB. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy comparable entries due to absence in HPB record; this minor adaptation does not reduce overall confidence to medium.
notes: 1. ZERO-MACRO JUSTIFICATION: carbohydrate_g is reported as 0.0 g/100g and sugar_g as 0.0 g/100g per the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this specific dish. This is anomalous given that wonton wrappers are made from refined wheat flour; it likely reflects the analytical result for the specific HPB sample where the sauce-coated filling dominated the tested portion. These values are confirmed zero per the approved HPB source and are preserved verbatim. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 g/100g — confirmed zero per HPB source, consistent with refined wheat flour wrappers with no vegetable additions. 2. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES (-1): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions and micronutrient data for this specific Singaporean dry-tossed wonton dish are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 3. SATURATED FAT: estimated at 3.2 g/100g from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for comparable shrimp-pork dumpling preparations (blended estimate from USDA #21349 and #21350); HPB does not report saturated fat for this entry. 4. GI NOTE: HPB reports carbohydrate as 0.0 g/100g; glycemic load per serving therefore calculates to 0.0. GI of 55 assigned as analogous estimate for wheat-flour wonton wrappers per University of Sydney GI Database. Per standard GI classification (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70), GI 55 is classified as low. gi_category corrected from 'medium' to 'low' in iteration 3 to align with GI value of 55. 5. HALAL STATUS: dish contains pork and is not halal.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' per G5 hard gate requirements.

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' to match glycemic_index value of 55. Per standard GI classification scale (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70), GI 55 falls in the low category. Updated gi_source notes and record notes to document this correction.
